content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 268 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_004_page_001.jpg

### Living for God, Not for Human Desires

1. Since Christ suffered in his physical body, you must arm yourselves with the same attitude he had. For if you have suffered physically for Christ, you have finished with sin.

2. You will no longer spend the rest of your earthly lives chasing after human desires, but instead will live for what God wants.

3. You have already spent enough time in the past doing what the pagans love to do — living in wild partying, unbridled lusts, drunken binges, orgies, drinking bouts, and disgusting idol worship.

4. Of course, your former friends are surprised that you no longer plunge into the same flood of reckless, destructive living with them. So they slander you.

5. But they will have to give an account to the one who stands ready to judge both the living and the dead.

6. This is why the Good News was preached even to those who are now dead — so that although they were judged in the body as all humans are, they might live in the spirit as God does.

### Using God's Gifts Faithfully

7. The end of all things is near. So be clear-minded and self-controlled, so that you can pray effectively.

8. Above all, love each other deeply, because love covers over a multitude of sins.

9. Offer hospitality to one another without grumbling about it.

10. Each of you has received a gift from God. Use it to serve one another, as faithful managers of God's grace in its many forms.

11. If anyone speaks, they should do so as one delivering God's very words. If anyone serves, they should do so with the strength God provides, so that in everything God may be praised through Jesus Christ. To him belong the glory and the power forever and ever. Amen.

### Suffering as a Christian

12. Dear friends, do not be surprised by the fiery ordeal that has come upon you to test you, as though something strange were happening to you.

13. Instead, be glad that you are sharing in the sufferings of Christ, so that when his glory is revealed you may be overjoyed.

14. If you are insulted because you bear the name of Christ, consider yourselves blessed, because the Spirit of glory — the Spirit of God — rests upon you.

15. Of course, if you suffer, it should not be as a murderer, a thief, a criminal, or as a meddler in other people's affairs.

16. However, if you suffer because you are a Christian, do not be ashamed. Instead, praise God that you bear that name.

17. For the time has come for judgment to begin, and it will begin with God's own household. And if judgment starts with us first, what will be the fate of those who refuse to obey God's Good News?

18. And if it is hard enough for the righteous person to be saved, what will become of the ungodly and the sinner?

19. So then, those who suffer according to God's will should entrust their lives to their faithful Creator and continue doing what is right.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "arm yourselves with the same attitude":** The Greek (ennoian) means "thought/mindset." The Sinaiticus reads clearly here. Dynamic rendering captures the military metaphor (hoplisasthe = "arm yourselves") combined with a mental disposition, conveying readiness for spiritual struggle.

**Verse 3 — Catalog of vices:** The Sinaiticus text lists: aselgeiais (debaucheries), epithumiais (lusts), oinophlugiais (drunkenness), komois (carousing), potois (drinking parties), and athemitais eidololatriais (unlawful idolatries). These are rendered with modern equivalents that capture the excessive and socially destructive character of each behavior.

**Verse 6 — "preached even to those who are now dead":** This is one of the most debated verses in the epistle. The Sinaiticus reads nekrois eueggelisthe (the dead were evangelized). The dynamic rendering clarifies the theological intent: the purpose (hina) clause sets up a contrast between physical death (judgment in the body) and spiritual life (living in the spirit according to God). This likely refers to believers who heard the gospel while alive but have since died.

**Verse 8 — "love covers over a multitude of sins":** An echo of Proverbs 10:12. The Sinaiticus reads agape kaluptei plethos hamartion. In dynamic equivalence, this conveys that genuine, deep love within the community fosters forgiveness and reconciliation rather than keeping score of offenses.

**Verse 11 — Doxology:** The Sinaiticus text includes the doxology formula "to whom is the glory and the power into the ages of the ages, amen" (hoi estin he doxa kai to kratos eis tous aionas ton aionon amen). The antecedent of "him" is debated (God or Jesus Christ); the Sinaiticus grammar most naturally points to Jesus Christ as the nearest referent.

**Verse 14 — "Spirit of glory... Spirit of God":** The Sinaiticus reads to tes doxes kai to tou theou pneuma. This double description emphasizes that the Holy Spirit who rests on persecuted believers is both the Spirit of divine glory and the Spirit of God himself. Some later manuscripts add additional phrases here, but the Sinaiticus text is more concise.

**Verse 16 — "Christian":** The Greek Christianos appears only three times in the NT (here, Acts 11:26, Acts 26:28). The Sinaiticus spells it clearly. By Peter's time, the label that outsiders gave to believers had become a recognized identity — and potentially a legal charge. The dynamic rendering preserves this specific term since it carries direct meaning in modern English.

**Verse 17 — Judgment beginning at God's household:** The Sinaiticus reads oikos tou theou (house of God), meaning the community of believers. The rhetorical argument moves from lesser to greater: if even God's own people face purifying judgment, the outcome for those who reject God's message will be far worse.

**Verse 18 — Proverbs 11:31 allusion:** Peter quotes from the Septuagint version of Proverbs 11:31. The Sinaiticus text reads ei ho dikaios molis sozetai, ho asebes kai hamartolos pou phaneitai ("if the righteous person is scarcely saved, where will the ungodly and sinner appear?"). The dynamic rendering captures the rhetorical force of this sobering question.
